focus: Intonation for Emphasis & Contrast — Pitch Patterns That Carry Meaning
focus_ar: التنغيم للتوضيح والتناقض — أنماط نبرة الصوت التي تحمل معنى
arabic_challenge: Beyond word stress (L1 Week 1), native English speakers use PITCH MOVEMENT (rising and falling tone across a phrase) to signal emphasis, contrast, sarcasm, and correction — often without changing a single word. Arabic intonation exists but maps meaning differently, so Arabic-accented English sometimes sounds flat or emotionally mismatched even when grammar and word stress are both correct.

target_sounds: contrastive emphasis: shifting stress to correct or contrast information

isolation.instructions: When correcting or contrasting a specific piece of information, native speakers shift extra pitch and stress onto exactly that word — even a normally unstressed word.
isolation.instructions_ar: لما تصحح أو تناقض معلومة معينة، المتكلم الأصلي بينقل نبر ونبرة صوت زيادة بالضبط على هذه الكلمة — حتى لو كانت كلمة عادةً بدون نبر.
practice: I said BLUE, not green. (contrast on 'blue')
SHE did it, not him. (contrast on 'she')
I meant TODAY, not tomorrow. (contrast on 'today')

pair: I said BLUE, not green. (contrastive stress)
I said blue, not green. (flat, no emphasis)

sentence_practice: I said BLUE, not green — please listen carefully.
SHE did it, not him — get your facts straight.
I meant TODAY, not tomorrow, when I made the plan.
daily_drills.record_this: I said BLUE, not green — SHE did it, not him — and I meant TODAY, not tomorrow!
daily_drills.self_check: Did the contrasted word clearly stand out with extra pitch and stress, more than in normal, neutral speech?

target_sounds: rising intonation for genuine surprise or disbelief

isolation.instructions: A sharp rise in pitch on a key word signals surprise, disbelief, or genuine question — even on a statement that isn't grammatically a question.
isolation.instructions_ar: ارتفاع حاد في نبرة الصوت على كلمة معينة بيدل على استغراب أو عدم تصديق أو سؤال حقيقي — حتى لو الجملة نحويًا مش سؤال.

sentence_practice: You did WHAT? I can't believe it!
daily_drills.record_this: You did WHAT? I can't believe it! She said WHAT? That's shocking — really? No WAY!
daily_drills.self_check: Did your pitch genuinely rise sharply on the key word, conveying real surprise, not a flat reading?

target_sounds: falling intonation for confident, final statements

isolation.instructions: A strong, falling pitch at the end of a statement signals confidence and finality — useful for presentations, debates, and firm opinions.
isolation.instructions_ar: نبرة صوت منخفضة قوية في نهاية الجملة بتدل على ثقة وحسم — مفيدة في العروض التقديمية والنقاشات والآراء القوية.
practice: This is the right decision. (firm, falling)
I strongly believe this is true. (firm, falling)

pair: This is the right decision. (falling, confident)
This is the right decision? (rising, unsure)

sentence_practice: This is the right decision, and I strongly believe it.
This is the final answer — there's no doubt.
I strongly believe this is the right decision.
daily_drills.record_this: This is the right decision, and I strongly believe it — this is the final answer, there's no doubt.
daily_drills.self_check: Did your pitch fall confidently at the end of each statement, rather than trailing off unsurely or rising into a question?

target_sounds: intonation in debate: emphasis to strengthen an argument

isolation.instructions: In debate-style speech (common in L2's speaking missions), strategic emphasis on key argument words makes your position sound more persuasive.
isolation.instructions_ar: في الكلام على شكل نقاش (شائع في مهام الكلام في المستوى الثاني)، التركيز الاستراتيجي على كلمات الحجة الأساسية بيخلي موقفك أكتر إقناعًا.
practice: This is EXACTLY why we need change. (emphasis on 'exactly')
The evidence CLEARLY shows this. (emphasis on 'clearly')

sentence_practice: This is EXACTLY why we need change.
The evidence CLEARLY shows this is true.
The FACT is, we MUST consider all options.
daily_drills.record_this: This is EXACTLY why we need change — the evidence CLEARLY shows this is true, and the FACT is, we MUST consider all options.
daily_drills.self_check: In an argumentative tone, did your emphasis land forcefully on the key persuasive words?

target_sounds: combining emphasis, surprise, and confident intonation in spontaneous speech

isolation.instructions: Practice a spontaneous 30-45 second speech (agree or disagree with a statement) that naturally requires all three intonation patterns from this week.
isolation.instructions_ar: تدرب على كلام عفوي لمدة ٣٠-٤٥ ثانية (توافق أو ترفض تصريح) بيحتاج طبيعيًا الأنماط الثلاثة من هذا الأسبوع.
practice: Respond to: 'Working from home is always better than working in an office.' Agree or disagree, with genuine emphasis and conviction.

pair: spontaneous speech with natural intonation variety

daily_drills.record_this: (Free speech: respond to the working-from-home statement for 30-45 seconds, using contrastive, surprised, and confident intonation naturally.)
daily_drills.self_check: Did your pitch vary naturally and meaningfully, or did it stay flat throughout your response?

daily_drills.target_sounds: Review: contrastive emphasis, rising surprise, falling confidence, debate emphasis, spontaneous variety
challenge_sentences: You did WHAT? I said BLUE, not green — this is EXACTLY why I don't trust that plan!
SHE did it, not him — and this is the right decision, no doubt about it!
Really? The evidence CLEARLY shows this is the final answer — I strongly believe it!
daily_drills.record_this: You did WHAT? I said BLUE, not green — this is EXACTLY why I don't trust that plan! SHE did it, not him — and this is the right decision, no doubt about it!
daily_drills.self_check: This passage requires switching between 3 different intonation patterns quickly. Did each one come through distinctly?

test_yourself.instructions: Record a 60-second spontaneous mini-debate defending a position, naturally using contrastive emphasis, surprise (if reacting to a counter-argument), and confident falling intonation for your conclusion.
test_yourself.passage: (Free speech: argue for or against 'Social media should have a minimum age requirement of 16.' Include at least one correction/contrast, and end with a confident, falling-intonation conclusion.)

scoring_guide.90_100: Intonation patterns used naturally and appropriately throughout, clearly conveying emphasis, contrast, and confidence.
scoring_guide.70_89: Most patterns present; a few moments of flat, unvaried pitch.
scoring_guide.50_69: Patterns present only in drilled sentences, largely flat in spontaneous speech.
scoring_guide.below_50: Consistently flat/monotone delivery — go back and repeat Days 1-4, exaggerating the pitch changes at first.

for_arabic_speakers: This week builds on L1's basic intonation patterns (statements falling, questions rising) by adding DELIBERATE, MEANINGFUL pitch shifts that native speakers use constantly in real conversation and argument.

It's fine — even encouraged — to exaggerate these patterns at first. Native speakers' real intonation range is often wider than learners expect; underdoing it is a more common error than overdoing it.
This directly supports L2's frequent debate and presentation-style speaking missions, where flat intonation makes even a well-argued point sound unconvincing.
supplementary_notes.recording_requirement: Record Day 5 and Day 7's spontaneous speech — compare your pitch variety here to your L1 recordings to track genuine growth in expressiveness.
